attributes.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for review after an episode of acute angle closure glaucoma. The episode responded rapidly to acetazolamide and topical pilocarpine. She reports three previous episodes, all of which resolved spontaneously. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her intraocular pressure is 25 mmHg (12-22).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate long-term management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with recurrent episodes of acute angle closure glaucoma with persistently elevated intraocular pressure after resolution of the acute episode. This indicates the need for definitive treatment rather than continued medical management.

Peripheral iridotomy is the first-line definitive treatment for angle closure glaucoma. This laser procedure creates a small hole in the peripheral iris, allowing aqueous humor to flow from the posterior chamber to the anterior chamber, bypassing the pupillary block that causes angle closure. It prevents recurrent attacks and is the standard of care for long-term management of angle closure glaucoma. Of note, this may work well in Caucasian populations, but in Asian eyes (with more iris pigmentation) this procedure is not as successful in controlling the intra-ocular pressure. In those racial groups, phacoemulsification and intraocular lens placement may be more effective - but more technically challenging.

Pilocarpine drops are useful in the acute setting to constrict the pupil and pull the peripheral iris away from the trabecular meshwork, but they are not appropriate for long-term management as they do not address the underlying anatomical problem and can cause side effects including headache, reduced vision in dim light, and myopia.

Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces aqueous humor production and is effective for emergency management of acute angle closure, but it is not suitable for long-term use due to systemic side effects including paresthesias, fatigue, and metabolic acidosis.

Topical timolol, a beta-blocker, reduces aqueous humor production and can be used to control intraocular pressure, but it does not address the underlying mechanism of angle closure and would not prevent future attacks. It may be used as adjunctive therapy but is not the definitive treatment.

Trabeculectomy is a filtering surgery that creates an alternative pathway for aqueous humor outflow. Trabeculectomy alone is not recommended as the success rate in patients who have not responded to medical therapy is about 50%. It is typically reserved for cases where intraocular pressure cannot be controlled with less invasive methods or where there is progressive glaucomatous damage despite maximum medical therapy. It carries more risks than peripheral iridotomy and would not be the first-line approach for this patient.
learningPoints: Peripheral iridotomy is the first-line definitive treatment for acute angle closure glaucoma after initial medical management.
Recurrent episodes of angle closure require definitive surgical intervention rather than continued medical therapy.
Intraocular pressure above normal range after an acute episode indicates the need for definitive treatment.
references: Chan PP,  Pang JC, Tham CC. Acute primary angle closure–treatment strategies, evidences and economical considerations. Eye 2109; 33:110-19.
